Costa Rica Regenerativa

A holistic development initiative launched in 2018 that focuses on demonstrating regenerative practices as viable alternatives to extractive systems, shifting from passive to participatory societies,

Case Study
Y
links
https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE

33314415-10e1-81dd-9707-f60321ea6276, 33314415-10e1-81aa-8f90-ce5b7de45607, 33314415-10e1-814f-a6c8-cca8058b7b45, 33314415-10e1-81e9-b24a-efefcca7816a, 33314415-10e1-812c-9920-f7c3851cfdc4
type
organization
location_lat
10.4
background_section
## Background Costa Rica’s global reputation for conservation and carbon neutrality sits uneasily beside Guanacaste’s reality: [380,000 cattle hectares](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE) after four hundred years of ranching, heavy pesticide use, and soil loss—[75% of national soil](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE) already gone, [95% loss by 2050](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE) projected, with brown river plumes visible far offshore after rain. Latin America and the Caribbean, one of Earth’s most biodiverse regions, have [lost 94% abundance](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE). As Eduardo Muñoz [puts it](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE), the crisis is less about charismatic megafauna than about the sheer volume of life slipping away: "So when people look at me with squared eyes—oh, it's not about the panda bear, it's not about the polar bear—no, it's about the amount of life that we're losing." ### Origins and Network Costa Rica Regenerativa began in [2018](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE) to show regeneration on the ground—not only in theory—at a scale that could eventually reach the country. Muñoz [frames the aim](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE) as building a future from abundance rather than scarcity through regeneration: "What we're trying to do is build a better future and a future that is based on abundance and not scarcity, and do this through regeneration." ### Philosophy & Approach The group’s mental model places a living Earth at the base of a torus and spiritual connection at the top—dimensions that make some academics uncomfortable but that Muñoz treats as [necessary for any transition](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE): lasting change needs the ongoing integration of nature with a sense of something larger than ourselves. Regenerative development, in their usage, is holistic rather than reductionist: benefits for many kinds of life, measured in [hope beyond GDP metrics](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE)—hope, education, transparency, quality, peace, and conditions that enable life, not only minimized harm. A core shift is from “humans and nature” to [humans as nature](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE)—simple to say, hard to live, and prerequisite to real progress. They argue for [local cooperative democracy](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE) in place of democracy-as-usual that no longer delivers, and for bringing in youth—especially young women—while moving aside exhausted political styles. Culture is [identity glue, not spectacle](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE), and it is eroding faster than biodiversity. The “feminine principle” ties [food, care, and heritage](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE) to how landscapes are treated. Philosophically they move from a vertical, human-centered hierarchy toward an ecosystem frame and then toward a [life-centered ethic](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE)—knowledge, technology, and wisdom in service of life, reversing the old order. Responsibility for that future is [collective, not ministers](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE); it sits with people on the ground.

[object Object]
place
Guanacaste, Costa Rica
activities
Community Food Security Project|Savory Hub Initiative|Regenerative Agriculture Program|Educational Hive|MRV Collective Development
description
A holistic development initiative launched in 2018 that focuses on demonstrating regenerative practices as viable alternatives to extractive systems, shifting from passive to participatory societies, upholding feminine principles and cultural preservation, and supporting locally led decision-making and inclusive economic models.
id
costa-rica-regenerativa
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy Costa Rica Regenerativa keeps demonstrating regeneration across food, grasslands, education, and measurement—often [ahead of government](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE), regrettably, but visibly enough to persuade. Muñoz [ends on intergenerational urgency](https://youtu.be/teEfB0Q-xpE): hope that humanity can still change course in time, that a new human–nature relationship is possible, and a father’s question—whether his son will be ready for that future. The initiative continues to show that restoring land, communities, and biodiversity can be practical, healing, and economically credible—not optional.
website
https://www.costaricaregenerativa.org/
bioregional_context
The Guanacaste region comprises approximately 380,000 hectares of primarily cattle land, with a history of livestock farming spanning 400 years. The area faces significant environmental challenges, including being a world leader in pesticide use per capita, with 64% of fresh vegetables containing unsafe pesticide levels. The region has experienced substantial soil degradation, losing approximately 200 tons of topsoil per hectare annually.

Bioregional Organizer

Costa Rica Regenerativa
